items.fix: Use `sorted_list = sorted(my_list)` or call `my_list.sort()` for its side effect and continue using `my_list`.
items.why: `list.sort()` returns `None`; the bug presents later when something tries to iterate `None`.
items.example:
```python
asc = sorted(rows, key=lambda r: r.id)   # new list
rows.sort(key=lambda r: r.id)             # in place; returns None
```

items.mistake: Full-sorting just to take the first k.
items.why: Full sort is O(n log n); a size-k heap is O(n log k).
items.example:
```python
import heapq
top = heapq.nlargest(10, chunks, key=lambda c: c.score)
```

items.mistake: Relying on a non-stable sort to preserve insertion order on ties.
items.fix: Use Python's built-in (Timsort is stable). For other libraries, check the docs or sort the tiebreaker key first.
items.why: Equal-keyed rows can swap; unit tests pass because the lazy order *happens* to match — until they don't.
items.example:
```python
# Stable two-pass:
rows.sort(key=lambda r: r.id)     # tiebreaker first
rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r.score)  # primary; Timsort keeps id order within ties
```

items.mistake: Sorting a mixed-type list and expecting it to work.
items.fix: Coerce or filter so all elements share a comparable type.
items.why: Python 3 forbids `<` between unrelated types (`str` vs `int`); the sort raises `TypeError`.
items.example:
```python
# sorted([1, 'two', 3])   # TypeError
sorted([1, 'two', 3], key=str)   # cast first
```

items.mistake: Re-sorting on every insert into a growing list.
items.fix: Use `sortedcontainers.SortedList` (O(log n) insert) or maintain a heap.
items.why: Each `xs.append(); xs.sort()` is O(n log n); doing this in a loop is O(n² log n).
items.example:
```python
from sortedcontainers import SortedList
sl = SortedList()
for x in stream(): sl.add(x)   # O(log n) per insert
```
